attributes.stem: A 28-year-old Aboriginal woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-week history of an itchy rash on her left forearm. She lives in a remote community with members of her immediate and extended family. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits and she is afebrile. There is a 5 cm circular, scaly patch with a raised, erythematous border on her left forearm. The rest of her skin examination is unremarkable. A skin scraping is positive for dermatophytes.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a classic case of tinea /tinea corporis. The characteristic features include a circular, scaly patch with a raised, erythematous border, and the diagnosis is confirmed by the positive skin scraping for dermatophytes. Given that this is a localised infection in an otherwise healthy individual, topical treatment is most appropriate.

Topical terbinafine is the most appropriate treatment for this patient. It is a highly effective antifungal agent that works by inhibiting squalene epoxidase, an enzyme necessary for fungal cell membrane synthesis. Terbinafine has both fungicidal and fungistatic properties and is effective against a wide range of dermatophytes. It is typically applied regularly for one to two weeks.

Topical hydrocortisone is not appropriate for treating tinea corporis. While it might temporarily reduce inflammation and itching, it does not address the underlying fungal infection. Moreover, corticosteroids can potentially worsen fungal infections by suppressing the local immune response.

Oral ivermectin is an antiparasitic medication primarily used to treat various parasitic infections including scabies. It is not effective against fungal infections and would not be appropriate for treating tinea corporis.

Topical permethrin is an insecticide used to treat scabies and lice infestations. It has no antifungal properties and would not be effective against tinea corporis.

Oral fluconazole is an antifungal medication that can be used to treat various fungal infections, including more severe or widespread cases of tinea. However, for a localised tinea corporis infection in an immunocompetent patient, topical treatment is generally preferred due to its efficacy and lower risk of systemic side effects.

It is worth noting that the patient lives with other family members which may lead to increased risk of transmission. Education about hygiene, avoiding sharing personal items, and possibly screening family members for similar infections might be beneficial in preventing spread and recurrence.
learningPoints: Tinea corporis is a common fungal skin infection that presents as an itchy, scaly patch with a raised border.
Topical terbinafine is an effective first-line treatment for localized tinea corporis in immunocompetent patients.
Close living conditions can increase the risk of transmission of fungal skin infections.
references: Kovitwanichkanont T, Chong A. Superficial fungal infections. Aust Fam Physician. 2019 Oct 1;48(10):706–11. Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2019/october/superficial-fungal-infections

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
